author: @DownhillAllTheWay
text: With constant angular velocity of the input shaft, the output shaft does not have constant angular velocity, so this kind of joint can only be used for applications that accept a non-linear velocity feed.  That's why transmission drives on front-wheel drive cars use CVJ's instead of UJ's (Constant Velocity Joints instead of Universal Joints).  UJ's are used on the drive shaft of rear-wheel drive cars, between the gearbox and the diff, but there are two UJ's - one at the gearbox output and another one feeding into the differential, and as the two feeds are as nearly parallel as possible, the two UJ's are used 90 degrees out of phase, so that the non-linearity of one cancels the non-linearity of the other - but UJ's cannot be used on a front-wheel drive car, where power is transferred through the joint while the car wheel is following a bend.

replies.author: @west_park7993
replies.text: uj=cardan italian mathematician cardano

replies.author: @DownhillAllTheWay
replies.text: @west_park7993 UJ = Universal Joint.

replies.author: @west_park7993
replies.text: @DownhillAllTheWay goto wiki. cardan shaft is how is popular in east europe. there are some cool animation and some trigonometry that shows the uneven output motion.

replies.author: @DownhillAllTheWay
replies.text: @west_park7993 Thanks for the suggestion, but if you want somebody to find a web site, give them a link - than they don't have to spend time searching.  You say "goto Wiki" - but Wiki is a big place!

replies.author: @DownhillAllTheWay
replies.text:
@west_park7993 OK, I looked it up with a Google search.  It says "What is a Cardan shaft?
1. : a shaft that has a *_universal joint_* at one or both ends enabling it to rotate freely when in varying angular relation to another shaft or shafts to which it is joined. 
2. : a shaft (as on a motor vehicle) that transmits power.

You clearly didn't understand the comment you were replying to.  "UJ's are used on the drive shaft of rear-wheel drive cars, between the gearbox and the diff"
These are NOT the kind of drive that is the subject of the video - the spherical coulisse drive - whose output has a roughly sinosoidal angular velocity if the drive has constant agular velocity.

replies.author: @chazbenningfield3743
replies.text: If there was any counter acting force then it would freely spin once the arm is at the center top. This could be useful for objects that could be both powered to rotate or rotated by hand without using a disengaging mechanism. However the transfer will lock if if the rim happens to align perpendicular to the path of the arm connection.

replies.author: @teardowndan5364
replies.text: @chazbenningfield3743 My point was that if there is a load on the output, then the drive arm has to pass through the "neutral" region really quickly to prevent counter-rotation from output load causing the arm to smash into the track at a significant angle with all of the associated shock loads.

That's sort of the problem with typical U-joints at a good sized angle, the output doesn't turn smoothly but sort of speeds up and slows down as it goes once around (hence the need for different CV (constant-velocity) joints in front-wheel drive cars.

Seems like beveled gears are better.

author: @ferrumignis
text: It's not a constant velocity mechanism unfortunately.  The output shaft speed looks like a raised cosine, hitting zero every 180 degrees so there would be huge torsional vibration for actual power transfer, though there might be specific applications for this. It looks like the  3D model needs some adjutsing, the output shaft isn't centred correctly.

author: @MrSaemichlaus
text: That's a very neat gadget, I would totally have it as a desktop toy. I haven't seen it implemented in anything I can remember. Maybe it could be used to make specific adjustments in machinery within a limited range, or the singularity (dead spot) could be used to disengage the drive and allow manual input, but I can't imagine it being used for any continuous, high speed or high load power transmission applications. For practical purposes, the output is non-linear, non-determinate with the mentioned singularity, the bearing slides on a curved track which is inherently very expensive to produce and hardly serviceable, the loads on all the parts are complex and highly inconsistent. Perhaps the singularity could be eliminated by transforming it into a Tousi-couple or "nothing grinder" which has several tracks/sliders. I see you can adjust the geometry for angles from collinear to about 60°, but a cardan joint (universal joint) does the same thing and is adjustable during operation.

author: @Skinflaps_Meatslapper
text:
Aww, kind of unfortunate that it's not also a constant velocity joint in that configuration, as it would certainly be an interesting power transfer to tinker with otherwise.
I guess one interesting way to use it would be if the bottom portion of the drive were constantly being driven, say from a water wheel or engine, and you needed to drive something intermittently.  This could potentially allow you to engage and disengage power transmission by stopping the yoke at top dead center when no power input is needed.  You'd only get one full rotation out of it before it stopped, unless you used a flywheel to drive it past top dead center again.  Might have been useful for something like a blacksmith power hammer, in which the user would move the ram downward slightly by hand or foot pedal to get it out of that top dead center "rut" to have it power one complete hammer rotation, with each successive blow being commanded by the user.  Perhaps a pawl that stopped the upper portion right at top dead center each time, I dunno, it could work?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @bpark10001
text: That action has a singularity in its motion.  That makes it prone to jamming & having high friction.  Mechanical transmissions should be designed to maximize pure push/pull & rotary motions to minimize friction & jamming.

replies.author: @TonyWeirPD
replies.text: Perhaps a small flywheel on the output shaft would sort that out?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @bpark10001
replies.text:
@TonyWeirPD No it won't!  it may hide the problem under some VERY steady-state condition or very specific dynamic condition (where the flywheel "coasts through" the singularity), but will only make the condition more severe under different dynamic conditions.  Systems should be designed to NEVER enter jamming conditions due to singularities unless it is designed to jam (such as toggle-latch mechanism).
          Pressure angles for moving parts should be kept far away from "jamming condition" (where pressure angle exceeds arctangent (coefficient of friction).  Examples are (non-circular) gears with too high pressure angle, cams with excessively steep ascending angles, especially when the follower is in sliding contact.

author: @NoosaHeads
text: But what's the benefit of these couplings over bevel gears? Wouldn't bevel gears have less friction and be easier to make?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: in tests these have been sown to be more efficient in some cases and not in others - like in pumps for example - but then that is always the way in mechanics - some olutions are not good for everything - they never are

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
- most probably ease of manufacture - cutting gears takes "incremental precision" = this geometry (most of the types demonstrated) could be hand carved - and will still work after "finishing"..

Variable angle couplings have been tricky with gears, and CV joints haven't always been readily  "manufacturable" (and have friction and lubricant losses) - lever systems can be angle variant (ok not constant velocity either in many cases).

replies.author: @NoosaHeads
replies.text: @kadmow Thank you! Ian

author: @colinvanful
text:
there are two real problems  with this   transfer system  
1    they do not   provide a constant velocity  [  speed up  and down   throught  the rotation ]
2   engineers would     use the system   that    has  the  minimal friction /  points of  wear  .  ie   bevel gears  .

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't want to be rude here mate but they are not problems - to be honest if you think they are problems then you are not applying the mechanism properly - speed up and down is an asset in some cases - engineers would use a system appropriate to the problem

author: @kenrowe167
text: Looks to me like the Coulisse mechanism transfers zero torque when the drive arm (ball bearing) is in the vertical position. So the torque is not constant throughout the rotation of the drive arm.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: indeed

author: @scotttovey
text:
The Spherical Coulisse Drive Mechanism in any application in which you need a 2:1 gear ratio from the output, while maintaining an anti-backlash feature.

If whatever you are driving, freezes or jerks back, that energy will not transmit back into the drive input device, thereby protecting that device.

You would not want to use this in a transport vehicles transmission however, as one may end up finding one's self traveling the wrong way on an incline.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: maybe a trip hammer or reciprocating saw?

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering 
That would work.
Or even a windshield wiper gearing.
actually, you may be able to hook the output arm of a windshield wiper motor directly to it with little modification.

author: @justanothercomment416
text: Just FYI, use some toothpaste as your lubricant. Operate the mechanism for a short duration. Wash it off. Then properly lube with some grease. You'll find it is now a polished, lubricated surface with much reduced friction. Works amazingly well on 3d printed gear sets too.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @hairyneil
replies.text: Interesting, here's hoping I remember this trick for when it's needed!

replies.author: @heathb4319
replies.text:
I also use toothpaste to polish my headlights to get the yellowing off of them.
Lot cheaper and works just fine.

replies.author: @hairyneil
replies.text: @heathb4319 I also need to do that! Better go get some engineering toothpaste...😂

author: @jamesross1003
text: I like this.  I am thinking that multiple arms on the output would add to your torque and drop speed.  Would be fun to play with though limited practical use.  Thanks for the video Rob!  Very much liking the good Doctor look and video showing different places where you live during commentary.  You get some exercise and we get to be tourists.  Win win!  Keep up the good work!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - cheers mate

replies.author: @curt2742
replies.text: If you did that you'd end up with restricted movement, and if you corrected for that restriction, you'd essentially have a bevel gear.

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @curt2742 Granted it would be close to a bevel gear, though I was talking about the upper arms.  Like a starfish legs.  An example of a flat version is the machine where there are multiple sliders in a block whose arms cause a leg to slide as the crank in spun.  Only rather than flat it rolls up and through the pivot point and progresses to the next arm.  It would actually give it a wider range of motion than a lower number of legs.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: Unfortunately the transfer of energy using this drive is not constant. At certain points in the rotation of the driving part you will find that the semi circle part will vary. The result is that if the output happened to have mass (such as a fly wheel ) the mechanism may shake to pieces. But 8n some cases the speeding up and slowing down in the output may actually be of benefit.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: like all mechanisms it is going to be good in some instances and not in others mate
